narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and personal circumstances to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 24 (well under the usual 36.70), and low year 1 and year 3 school decile rankings of 1—and predicted a 96% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk directly led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar and graduate without issues. The three most influential factors in this decision were your year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your year 1 school decile ranking, all of which strongly pushed the prediction toward failure rather than success. Your year 3 decile of 1, placing you in the bottom tenth of your school, had the single biggest negative impact, signaling to the model a significant drop-off in performance that often correlates with bar struggles. Similarly, your low LSAT score weighed heavily against you, as it deviated sharply from the higher scores typical of students who pass the bar. Even your year 1 decile of 1 contributed further in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about early academic challenges that persisted. Compared to typical students, who often rank in the middle or higher deciles and score much stronger on the LSAT, your metrics stood out as unusually weak predictors of bar passage. While factors like planning to attend full-time (the norm for 93% of students) didn't sway things much, they couldn't offset these core academic red flags. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment aimed at the school's outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and targeting schools with more flexible admissions while building a standout personal statement highlighting your resilience. With targeted improvements, you can reshape how models like this view your bar exam prospects and open new doors.
